title: Cat Liquid Yellow Diarrhea: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's diarrhea liquid and yellow? Learn about potential causes, the medium urgency level, and why a photo helps your vet with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow liquid diarrhea in cats is caused by rapid transit of bile through the intestines and poses a high risk of dehydration, requiring a veterinary visit within 24 hours or immediate emergency care if accompanied by vomiting or lethargy.

What does yellow liquid diarrhea mean for my cat?

  • Yellow diarrhea occurs when stool moves through the intestines so quickly that bile doesn't have time to break down properly.
  • The liquid consistency indicates significant water loss and a high risk of dehydration.
  • Potential underlying causes include inflammation, infection, or issues with the liver and gallbladder.

How quickly should I seek veterinary care if my cat has yellow liquid diarrhea?

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours for any episode of completely liquid yellow stool.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if your cat is also vomiting, refusing food, or acting extremely lethargic.

How can taking a photo of my cat's diarrhea help the veterinarian?

  • A clear photo allows the vet to see the specific yellow pigment and the presence of any mucus.
  • Visual details help determine if the issue is originating in the small intestine.
  • Seeing undigested material can help your vet identify specific parasites, such as Giardia, that might be the cause.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Diarrhea in cats can be associated with various underlying conditions. In cases of severe diarrhea, physical examination may reveal profound depression, dehydration, and sometimes abdominal pain. Abdominal palpation may induce immediate vomiting and reveal thickened intestinal loops and enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes. Diagnostic workup should include serologic tests for feline leukemia virus and feline immunodeficiency virus, as they can be associated with chronic diarrhea and are important prognostic factors. Feline infectious peritonitis and toxoplasmosis have also been described as occasional causes of chronic diarrhea. Treatment should involve a highly digestible diet with restricted fat content to minimize secretory diarrhea. Oral prednisolone may be indicated in cases of intestinal disease with an inflammatory component. In more severe cases, chlorambucil may be necessary. Metronidazole is often given as an adjunctive treatment.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 797)

faqs.question: Can diet changes cause yellow diarrhea?
faqs.answer: Yes, a sudden change in food can lead to rapid transit in the gut, resulting in yellow liquid stool.

faqs.question: Is yellow diarrhea a sign of liver failure?
faqs.answer: While it can be a symptom of liver or gallbladder issues, it is more commonly associated with rapid digestion or bacterial imbalances.

faqs.question: Should I fast my cat if they have diarrhea?
faqs.answer: No, you should never fast a cat without specific instructions from your vet, as fasting can lead to a dangerous condition called hepatic lipidosis.
